***300: Rise of an Empire*** is a 2014 American [epic](/wiki/Epic_film "Epic film") [historical](/wiki/Historical_drama "Historical drama") [action film](/wiki/Action_film "Action film") directed by [Noam Murro](/wiki/Noam_Murro "Noam Murro") from a screenplay by [Zack Snyder](/wiki/Zack_Snyder "Zack Snyder") and [Kurt Johnstad](/wiki/Kurt_Johnstad "Kurt Johnstad"), based on the then-unpublished comic book limited series *[Xerxes](/wiki/Xerxes_(graphic_novel) "Xerxes (graphic novel)")* by [Frank Miller](/wiki/Frank_Miller "Frank Miller").[[a]](#cite_note-Xerxes-3) A sequel to *[300](/wiki/300_(film) "300 (film)")* (2006), it takes place before, during, and after the main events of that film, and is a fictionalized retelling of the [Battle of Artemisium](/wiki/Battle_of_Artemisium "Battle of Artemisium") and the [Battle of Salamis](/wiki/Battle_of_Salamis "Battle of Salamis") in the [Greco-Persian Wars](/wiki/Greco-Persian_Wars "Greco-Persian Wars").[[7]](#cite_note-8) The cast includes [Lena Headey](/wiki/Lena_Headey "Lena Headey"), [Peter Mensah](/wiki/Peter_Mensah "Peter Mensah"), [David Wenham](/wiki/David_Wenham "David Wenham"), [Andrew Tiernan](/wiki/Andrew_Tiernan "Andrew Tiernan"), [Andrew Pleavin](/wiki/Andrew_Pleavin "Andrew Pleavin"), and [Rodrigo Santoro](/wiki/Rodrigo_Santoro "Rodrigo Santoro") reprising their roles from the first film, alongside [Sullivan Stapleton](/wiki/Sullivan_Stapleton "Sullivan Stapleton"), [Eva Green](/wiki/Eva_Green "Eva Green"), [Hans Matheson](/wiki/Hans_Matheson "Hans Matheson"), and [Callan Mulvey](/wiki/Callan_Mulvey "Callan Mulvey").

*300: Rise of an Empire* was released theatrically on March 7, 2014, by [Warner Bros. Pictures](/wiki/Warner_Bros._Pictures "Warner Bros. Pictures").[[8]](#cite_note-comingsoon.net-9)[[9]](#cite_note-10) It received mixed reviews, with critics praising the action sequences, music, cinematography, visual effects and Green's performance but criticizing the story, Stapleton's performance, and overstylized gore. The film was a box-office success, grossing $337 million worldwide from a $110 million budget.[[6]](#cite_note-BOM-7)

## Plot

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=300:_Rise_of_an_Empire&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Plot")]

Queen Gorgo of [Sparta](/wiki/Sparta "Sparta") narrates about the [Battle of Marathon](/wiki/Battle_of_Marathon "Battle of Marathon"), in which King [Darius](/wiki/Darius_I "Darius I") of [Persia](/wiki/Achaemenid_Empire "Achaemenid Empire") was killed by [Themistocles](/wiki/Themistocles "Themistocles") of [Athens](/wiki/Classical_Athens "Classical Athens"). Darius's son, [Xerxes](/wiki/Xerxes_I_of_Persia "Xerxes I of Persia"), witnesses his father's death and is advised to never wage war against the Greeks. Darius's naval commander, [Artemisia](/wiki/Artemisia_I_of_Caria "Artemisia I of Caria"), persuades Xerxes to become a god and sends him on a journey through the desert. Xerxes reaches a cave and bathes in an otherworldly liquid, emerging as a "God-King". He returns to Persia and declares war on Greece to avenge his father.

As Xerxes' forces advance towards [Thermopylae](/wiki/Battle_of_Thermopylae "Battle of Thermopylae"), Themistocles meets with the council and convinces them to provide him with a fleet to engage the Persians at sea. Themistocles travels to Sparta to ask King Leonidas for help but is informed by Dilios that Leonidas is consulting the Oracle, and Gorgo is reluctant to side with Athens. Themistocles reunites with his old friend Scyllias, who infiltrated the Persian troops, and reveals Artemisia was born Greek but defected to Persia after her family was murdered by Greek [hoplites](/wiki/Hoplite "Hoplite"). A Persian emissary took her in and trained her, and she eventually rose to become a naval commander. Themistocles also learns that Leonidas has marched to fight the Persians with only 300 men.

Themistocles leads his fleet of fifty warships and several thousand men, which include Scyllias, Scyllias's son Calisto, and Themistocles' right-hand man Aeschylus to the [Aegean Sea](/wiki/Aegean_Sea "Aegean Sea"), starting the [Battle of Artemisium](/wiki/Battle_of_Artemisium "Battle of Artemisium"). They ram their ships into the Persian ships and attack them before retreating. The following day, the Greeks feign a retreat and lead the Persian ships into a crevice, where they become stuck. The Greeks attack the Persian ships from the cliffs above. Impressed with Themistocles, Artemisia brings him onto her ship and attempts to seduce him to rally the Persian side as her second-in-command, but he refuses her offer.

The Persians attack the Greek ships with tar and flame bombs, but an Athenian kills one of the Persians, who falls into the tar carrying a torch, damaging ships from both sides. Themistocles is thrown into the sea by an explosive carried onboard by a Persian swimmer, nearly drowns before being rescued by Aeschylus, and is by Scyllias's side as he succumbs to his arrow injuries inflicted by Artemisia in echo of King Darius's death. Believing Themistocles to be dead, Artemisia and her forces withdraw. After recovering from his injuries, Themistocles learns that only a few hundred of his warriors and six of his ships survived the disastrous attack.

Daxos, an Arcadian general, tells Themistocles that Leonidas and his 300 men were killed after [Ephialtes](/wiki/Ephialtes_of_Trachis "Ephialtes of Trachis") betrayed the Greeks to Xerxes. Themistocles returns to Athens and confronts Ephialtes, who reveals that Xerxes plans to attack and burn Athens. Ephialtes regrets his betrayal and welcomes death but is spared so he can warn Xerxes that the Greek forces are gathering at [Salamis](/wiki/Salamis_Island "Salamis Island"). Themistocles visits Gorgo in Sparta to ask for help, but Gorgo, mourning Leonidas's death, refuses. Before departing, Themistocles urges Gorgo to avenge Leonidas.

In Athens, Xerxes' army is laying waste when Ephialtes arrives to deliver Themistocles' message. Upon learning he is alive, Artemisia leaves to ready her navy for battle. Themistocles inspires all of his remaining forces to continue fighting. The remaining Greek ships charge into the Persian ships, beginning the decisive [Battle of Salamis](/wiki/Battle_of_Salamis "Battle of Salamis"). Themistocles and Artemisia engage in a duel, which ends in a stalemate.

Gorgo arrives at the battle along with ships from numerous Greek city-states including [Delphi](/wiki/Delphi "Delphi"), [Thebes](/wiki/Thebes,_Greece "Thebes, Greece"), [Olympia](/wiki/Olympia,_Greece "Olympia, Greece"), [Arcadia](/wiki/Arcadia_(Crete) "Arcadia (Crete)"), and Sparta, all united against the Persians. Xerxes, watching the battle from a cliff, turns back, acknowledging his naval defeat and continuing the march of his army. Artemisia attacks Themistocles, but he stabs and kills her. Accompanied by Dilios, Themistocles and Gorgo lead the entire Greek army against the Persians.

## Production

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=300:_Rise_of_an_Empire&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Production")]

### Development

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=300:_Rise_of_an_Empire&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Development")]

In June 2008, producers [Gianni Nunnari](/wiki/Gianni_Nunnari "Gianni Nunnari"), [Mark Canton](/wiki/Mark_Canton "Mark Canton"), and Bernie Goldmann revealed that work had begun on a sequel to *[300](/wiki/300_(film) "300 (film)")*.[[13]](#cite_note-14) [Legendary Pictures](/wiki/Legendary_Entertainment "Legendary Entertainment") announced that [Frank Miller](/wiki/Frank_Miller "Frank Miller"), who wrote the 1998 comic book limited series on which the film *300* was based, was writing a follow-up graphic novel, and [Zack Snyder](/wiki/Zack_Snyder "Zack Snyder"), co-screenwriter and director of *300*, was interested in directing the adaptation, but instead chose to develop and direct the Superman reboot *[Man of Steel](/wiki/Man_of_Steel_(film) "Man of Steel (film)")*.[[14]](#cite_note-15)[[15]](#cite_note-16) [Noam Murro](/wiki/Noam_Murro "Noam Murro") directed instead, while Snyder produced and co-wrote. The film was centered on the Greek leader [Themistocles](/wiki/Themistocles "Themistocles"), portrayed by Australian actor [Sullivan Stapleton](/wiki/Sullivan_Stapleton "Sullivan Stapleton").[[16]](#cite_note-17) During pre-production, the film was titled *300: Battle of Artemisium* (although this was widely misreported as "Battle of Artemisia");[[17]](#cite_note-empireonline1-18) the film was retitled *300: Rise of an Empire* in September 2012.[[18]](#cite_note-19)

### Filming

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=300:_Rise_of_an_Empire&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Filming")]

[Principal photography](/wiki/Principal_photography "Principal photography") commenced in early July 2012 at the [Nu Boyana Film](/wiki/Nu_Boyana_Film "Nu Boyana Film") Studios in [Sofia](/wiki/Sofia "Sofia"), [Bulgaria](/wiki/Bulgaria "Bulgaria").[[19]](#cite_note-20) Underwater greenscreen scenes were also filmed at Leavesden Studios.[[20]](#cite_note-21) On May 10, 2013, it was announced the film's release date would be pushed back from August 2, 2013, to March 7, 2014.[[8]](#cite_note-comingsoon.net-9)

### Music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=300:_Rise_of_an_Empire&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Music")]

Main article: [300: Rise of an Empire (soundtrack)](/wiki/300:_Rise_of_an_Empire_(soundtrack) "300: Rise of an Empire (soundtrack)")

The [film's score](/wiki/Film_score "Film score") was composed by [Junkie XL](/wiki/Junkie_XL "Junkie XL"), being the first film in an ongoing partnership with Snyder.[[21]](#cite_note-22) He attempted to research on the ancient Persian and Greek music instrumentation to match the time period and culture, while also being a fantasy film, he tried to blend the sounds with electronic instruments here and there.[[22]](#cite_note-:0-23) The album featuring Junkie XL's score was released by [WaterTower Music](/wiki/WaterTower_Music "WaterTower Music") on March 4, 2014.

## Reception

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=300:_Rise_of_an_Empire&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Reception")]

### Box office

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=300:_Rise_of_an_Empire&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Box office")]

*300: Rise of an Empire* grossed $106.6 million in North America and $231 million in other territories for a worldwide total of $337.6 million, against a production budget of $110 million.[[6]](#cite_note-BOM-7)

In North America, the film opened at number one in its first weekend with $45 million.[[23]](#cite_note-24) In its second weekend, the film dropped to number two, grossing an additional $19.2 million.[[24]](#cite_note-25) In its third weekend, the film dropped to number five, grossing $8.5 million.[[25]](#cite_note-26) In its fourth weekend, the film dropped to number nine, grossing $4.2 million.[[26]](#cite_note-27)

### Critical response

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=300:_Rise_of_an_Empire&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Critical response")]

Review aggregation website [Rotten Tomatoes](/wiki/Rotten_Tomatoes "Rotten Tomatoes") gives the film an approval rating of 45% based on 197 reviews, with an average rating of 5/10. The site's critical consensus reads, "It's bound to hit some viewers as an empty exercise in stylish gore, and despite a gonzo starring performance from Eva Green, *300: Rise of an Empire* is a step down from its predecessor."[[27]](#cite_note-28) On [Metacritic](/wiki/Metacritic "Metacritic"), the film has a score of 48 out of 100 score, based on 34 critics, indicating "mixed or average" reviews.[[28]](#cite_note-29) Audiences polled by [CinemaScore](/wiki/CinemaScore "CinemaScore") gave the film an average grade of "B" on an A+ to F scale, down from the A− received by its predecessor.[[29]](#cite_note-CinemaScore-30)

Several critics compared the film negatively to its predecessor. Todd Gilchrist of *[The Wrap](/wiki/The_Wrap "The Wrap")* wrote: "*Rise of an Empire* lacks director Snyder's shrewd deconstruction of cartoonish hagiography, undermining the glorious, robust escapism of testosterone-fueled historical reenactment with an underdog story that's almost too reflective to be rousing."[[30]](#cite_note-31) Nicolas Rapold of *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")* gave a mixed review: "The naval collisions and melees play out in panel-like renderings that are bold and satisfying for the first half-hour but lack the momentum and bombastic je ne sais quoi of *300*."[[31]](#cite_note-32) Todd McCarthy of *[The Hollywood Reporter](/wiki/The_Hollywood_Reporter "The Hollywood Reporter")* thought it was "more monochromatic and duller in appearance, lacking the bold reds and rich earth tones" of the earlier film.[[32]](#cite_note-33) Scott Foundas of *[Variety](/wiki/Variety_(magazine) "Variety (magazine)")* gave a positive review: "This highly entertaining time-filler lacks the mythic resonances that made *300* feel like an instant classic, but works surprisingly well on its own terms."[[12]](#cite_note-variety1-13) Soren Anderson of *[The Seattle Times](/wiki/The_Seattle_Times "The Seattle Times")* thought it "very impressive in its single-minded dedication to creating a moviegoing experience designed to totally engulf its audience".[[33]](#cite_note-34) James Rocchi of *[Film.com](/wiki/Film.com "Film.com")* gave the film a zero out of ten and called it "a 3D joke".[[34]](#cite_note-35)

[James Berardinelli](/wiki/James_Berardinelli "James Berardinelli") wrote, "The movie delivers all the necessary elements but their impact is dull."[[35]](#cite_note-36) Joe Neumaier of the *[New York Daily News](/wiki/New_York_Daily_News "New York Daily News")* thought it looked "like an ashen video game. It's even more muddy in IMAX and 3-D."[[36]](#cite_note-37) Colin Covert of the *[Star Tribune](/wiki/Star_Tribune "Star Tribune")* felt it "plays like a collaboration between the Marquis de Sade and Michael Bay. Or maybe the History Channel and the *Saw* franchise."[[37]](#cite_note-38) Guy Lodge of *[Time Out](/wiki/Time_Out_(magazine) "Time Out (magazine)")* wrote, "It's flesh and carnage that the audience is here to see, and Murro delivers it by the glistening ton, pausing only for stray bits of backstory."[[38]](#cite_note-39) Ann Hornaday of *[The Washington Post](/wiki/The_Washington_Post "The Washington Post")* said, "*Rise of an Empire* is no fun at all – even those famous six-pack abs from *300* seem to be missing a can or two."[[39]](#cite_note-40) In a negative review, Drew Hunt of the *[Chicago Reader](/wiki/Chicago_Reader "Chicago Reader")* wrote: "The slow-motion battle scenes are technically impressive and occasionally elegant, but there's enough machismo here to choke a thousand NFL locker rooms."[[40]](#cite_note-41) [Richard Roeper](/wiki/Richard_Roeper "Richard Roeper") called the film "A triumph of production design, costumes, brilliantly choreographed battle sequences and stunning CGI".[[41]](#cite_note-42)

Scott Bowles of *[USA Today](/wiki/USA_Today "USA Today")* gave the film two out of four stars: "For anyone looking for a sense of script (forget plausibility), *Empire* is a Trojan horse."[[42]](#cite_note-43) Betsy Sharkey of the *[Los Angeles Times](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Times "Los Angeles Times")* thought, "The spectacularly brutal fighting is the film's main calling card, and in that *Rise of an Empire* doesn't disappoint."[[43]](#cite_note-44) David Hiltbrand of *[The Philadelphia Inquirer](/wiki/The_Philadelphia_Inquirer "The Philadelphia Inquirer")* praised "its slo-mo ultraviolence" and "impressive 3-D effects", calling it "a fan boy's fantasy, a four-star wonderment".[[44]](#cite_note-45) Tom Long of *[The Detroit News](/wiki/The_Detroit_News "The Detroit News")* gave the film a D, "a bloodbath and not much else".[[45]](#cite_note-46) Adam Nayman of *[The Globe and Mail](/wiki/The_Globe_and_Mail "The Globe and Mail")* called it "an add-on content pack for a video game".[[46]](#cite_note-47) Mark Jenkins of [NPR](/wiki/NPR "NPR") gave the film a negative review, saying, "If the movie's action recalls video games, the dramatically artificial lighting suggests 1980s rock videos. Indeed, *Rise of an Empire* is so campy that it might work better as a musical."[[47]](#cite_note-48) Stephen Whitty of the *[Newark Star-Ledger](/wiki/Newark_Star-Ledger "Newark Star-Ledger")* gave the film one and a half stars out of four, saying, "There is much grinding of teeth, and mauling of history, and anachronistic use of gunpowder, until we plug our ears and desperately pray to the gods of Olympus, or the brothers of Warner, that they might make an end."[[48]](#cite_note-49)

Despite mixed reviews for the film as a whole, [Eva Green](/wiki/Eva_Green "Eva Green")'s performance as the naval leader [Artemisia](/wiki/Artemisia_I_of_Caria "Artemisia I of Caria") received positive reviews, with some going so far as to say she was more interesting than the heroes, and saved the film. [Ty Burr](/wiki/Ty_Burr "Ty Burr") of *[The Boston Globe](/wiki/The_Boston_Globe "The Boston Globe")* stated, "*Rise of an Empire* may strike some as an improvement on the first film, if only for two reasons: naval warfare and the glorious absurdity of Eva Green."[[49]](#cite_note-50) According to Rafer Guzman's *[Newsday](/wiki/Newsday "Newsday")* review, "The one bright spot is Eva Green as Xerxes' machinator, Artemesia [*[sic](/wiki/Sic "Sic")*], a raccoon-eyed warrior princess... Green plays a snarling, insatiable, self-hating femme fatale and completely steals the show."[[50]](#cite_note-51) Stephanie Zacharek writing for *[The Village Voice](/wiki/The_Village_Voice "The Village Voice")* exclaimed, "Mere mortals of Athens, Sparta, and every city from Mumbai to Minneapolis, behold the magnificent Eva Green, and tremble!"[[51]](#cite_note-52)

## Historical accuracy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=300:_Rise_of_an_Empire&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Historical accuracy")]

*[The Guardian](/wiki/The_Guardian_(newspaper) "The Guardian (newspaper)")'s* historical films reviewer, [Alex von Tunzelmann](/wiki/Alex_von_Tunzelmann "Alex von Tunzelmann"), discredited the film's historical legitimacy, giving it the classification of "History grade: Fail". She itemizes numerous historical discrepancies in the film, including the pivotal scene in which [Themistocles](/wiki/Themistocles "Themistocles") kills [Darius the Great](/wiki/Darius_the_Great "Darius the Great") at the [Battle of Marathon](/wiki/Battle_of_Marathon "Battle of Marathon"), although he was really absent and died of natural causes only years later.[[54]](#cite_note-von_tunzelmann_the_guardian-55) Tunzelmann further quotes the *[Persian Fire](/wiki/Persian_Fire "Persian Fire")* author and historian [Tom Holland](/wiki/Tom_Holland_(author) "Tom Holland (author)"), who translated [Herodotus's *Histories*](/wiki/Histories_(Herodotus) "Histories (Herodotus)"), and who is an expert on the [Greco-Persian wars](/wiki/Greco-Persian_wars "Greco-Persian wars"), as comparing the film to a wild fantasy substitute for actual historic reality.[[54]](#cite_note-von_tunzelmann_the_guardian-55)

[Paul Cartledge](/wiki/Paul_Cartledge "Paul Cartledge"), a professor of [Greek culture](/wiki/Ancient_Greece "Ancient Greece") at [Cambridge University](/wiki/Cambridge_University "Cambridge University"), also noted historical inaccuracies in the film. For example, Darius was not killed as depicted, and neither he nor [Xerxes](/wiki/Xerxes_I "Xerxes I") was present at the Battle of Marathon. [Artemisia](/wiki/Artemisia_I_of_Caria "Artemisia I of Caria"), historically a queen and not an abused orphaned slave, actually argued against sailing into the straits and survived the Persian Wars. In addition, the Spartan Navy contributed a mere 16 warships to the Greek fleet of 400 warships in the ending battle scene, rather than the huge armada shown.[[55]](#cite_note-56)[[56]](#cite_note-57)

Some critics have identified the film as an example of [Iranophobia](/wiki/Iranophobia "Iranophobia").[[57]](#cite_note-58) Tunzelmann found the film being the same "massive gilded embodiment of [orientalism](/wiki/Orientalism "Orientalism") from [its predecessor]".[[54]](#cite_note-von_tunzelmann_the_guardian-55)[[58]](#cite_note-Llewellyn-Jones-59) According to [Lloyd Llewellyn-Jones](/wiki/Lloyd_Llewellyn-Jones "Lloyd Llewellyn-Jones"), Professor of Ancient History at [Cardiff University](/wiki/Cardiff_University "Cardiff University"): "Indeed, the Persians of *300: Rise of an Empire* remain the incarnation of every Orientalist cliché imaginable: they are as decadent and oversexed as they are weak and spineless. They are also incapable of winning battles without the help of a Greek traitor: Artemisia, a woman who may be costumed like [Xena](/wiki/Xena "Xena"), a warrior princess, but whose heart is consumed by a crazy desire for power and destruction."[[58]](#cite_note-Llewellyn-Jones-59)According to Khabaronline, an Iranian media outlet, citing the evaluation by Khosrow Mo'tazed, the main message conveyed by this film is that the Iranian nation possesses no history or culture, and that everything originates from Greece[[59]](#cite_note-60).

## Home media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=300:_Rise_of_an_Empire&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Home media")]

*300: Rise of an Empire* was released on the [iTunes Store](/wiki/ITunes_Store "ITunes Store") on June 3, 2014, and was released on DVD and [Blu-ray](/wiki/Blu-ray "Blu-ray") three weeks later, on June 24.[[60]](#cite_note-61)

## Sequel

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=300:_Rise_of_an_Empire&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Sequel")]

In a 2016 interview, Snyder stated that more sequels to *300* would focus on topics beyond Ancient Greece, such as the [American Revolutionary War](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War"), the [Battle of the Alamo](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Alamo "Battle of the Alamo"), or a battle in [China](/wiki/China "China").[[61]](#cite_note-62)

In May 2021, Snyder revealed that he had written an [Alexander the Great](/wiki/Alexander_the_Great "Alexander the Great") film that was intended to function as a conclusion to the *300* trilogy, adapting the *Rise of Alexander* segment of *[Xerxes](/wiki/Xerxes_(graphic_novel) "Xerxes (graphic novel)")*, but it evolved into having a greater focus on a love story between Alexander and [Hephaestion](/wiki/Hephaestion "Hephaestion"), leading Snyder to think it could not function as a third *300* film, but rather a stand-alone entry in the same [fictional universe](/wiki/Fictional_universe "Fictional universe"). The script was retitled *Blood and Ashes* but it failed to be greenlit by Warner Bros. Pictures.[[62]](#cite_note-63)[[63]](#cite_note-64)

In December 2023, Snyder revealed that he had regained the rights for *Blood and Ashes* from Warner Bros. Pictures and was planning on developing the film in the future.[[64]](#cite_note-65)
